Deep Dive Training — June 18, 2025

The 3 Checkpoints

The exact questions. Why they're asked. What happens when you skip them.

Teamtailor US · SMB Sales Team

Last Session Recap

What We Covered

Why Process > Leads

68% of buyers don't want to talk to reps. 88% buy from trusted advisors. The Wall of Disconnect between buyer and seller.

The 5 Fatalities

Timing, Competition, Price, Trust, Product Fit — the only 5 reasons deals die. Every lost deal traces to one of these.

The 4D Process

Discovery → Diagnosis → Demonstrate → Decision. Not "1.5D." The doctor doesn't prescribe before diagnosing.

Today we go deeper. From the what to the how.

Today's Mission

The Buyer's Action Plan

11 questions across 3 checkpoints. Each one exists for a reason. Today you learn every question, why it's asked, and what breaks when you skip it.

Checkpoint 1
Urgency Test
Do they need to act?
→
Checkpoint 2
Gap Test
Do they need outside help?
→
Checkpoint 3
Solution Test
Are we the best option?
The Framework

The Buyer's Action Plan

11 questions · 3 checkpoints · Deals cannot advance past a checkpoint unless it scores green.

Checkpoint 1
Do They Need to Act?
Q1 · Their Objective
What is the problem that has been identified?
Q2 · Decision Makers
Who else cares about this?
Q3 · Current Reality
What have they tried and why hasn't it worked?
Q4 · Priority & Urgency
What's the worst outcome if not solved?
8-10 YES · 5-7 MAYBE · 1-4 NO
Checkpoint 2
Do They Need Outside Help?
Q5 · Other Providers
Are they currently using any providers you compete with?
Q6 · Problem Diagnosis
Why haven't existing resources been able to fix it?
Q7 · Buying Process
Who is involved in approving these purchases?
Q8 · Budget & Timelines
Are they reactive or proactive buyers?
8-10 YES · 5-7 MAYBE · 1-4 NO
Checkpoint 3
Do We Have the Best Solution?
Q9 · Proven Results
Demonstrate a proven history of solving their problem.
Q10 · A Clear Solution
Be clear on how our solution will solve their problems.
Q11 · Delivery & Timelines
Align on key delivery milestones and deliverables.
8-10 YES · 5-7 MAYBE · 1-4 NO

Each question is scored Y/N/Maybe. Deals cannot advance past a checkpoint unless it scores in the green. Your unknowns tell you exactly what to ask on your next call.

The Psychology

The Doctor's Mindset

How you ask is just as important as what you ask.

❌ The Pitcher

  • "We can fix that! Want a demo?"
  • Pitches before diagnosing
  • Talks 80%, listens 20%
  • Triggers sales resistance
  • Chases prospects

✅ The Doctor

  • "I'm not sure we're the right fit. Help me understand..."
  • Diagnoses before prescribing
  • Talks 20%, listens 80%
  • Willing to disqualify
  • Prospects pull toward you

"A doctor doesn't beg you to take medicine. They ask where it hurts. If you say 'nowhere,' they send you home. You should too."

Pipeline Creation

3 Waves — Setting the Meeting

Use when cold calling to overcome initial resistance and set a meeting.

WaveWhat You SayThe Psychology
1. Direct "Do you have a couple of minutes to talk about any existing goals or problems you have with your hiring process?" Most say "No, we're fine." This is expected.
2. Disbelief "Really? There aren't any future problems you're worried about or long-term goals you have for your recruiting team?" Surprise creates pause. They second-guess their "no" — if they continue to hold, go to Wave 3.
3. Expose "Oh ok... when was the last time you evaluated your hiring process or applied through your own careers page?" The trap. If it's been a while → they don't actually know the current state.

💡 The close: "Understood. Would it be valuable to you if we did a free assessment so you know the current state of your candidate experience?" — You've taken their two "No" responses and challenged them with a question that reveals they don't know enough to hold those positions.

Before Checkpoint 1

3 Waves — Running the Meeting

They showed up but say "just want information." Break through before the BAP starts.

WaveWhat You SayThe Psychology
1. Direct "Typically when I meet with people like you, there's either a problem they're trying to solve or a goal they're trying to achieve — which is it for you?" Most say "No, we're fine." This is expected. Wave 1 is a warm-up — not a closer.
2. Disbelief "Really? So there aren't any future problems you're worried about or long-term goals you have for your hiring process?" Social pressure through surprise. Act genuinely surprised. Creates a pause — makes them second-guess their "no."
3. Expose "Oh ok... when was the last time you applied for a job through your own careers page?" The trap. They said "we're fine" — but they haven't actually checked. They can't credibly hold that position.

💡 The key insight: You've taken their two "No" responses and challenged them with a question that reveals whether or not they know enough to hold those positions. If they haven't looked, they don't know — and now they know they don't know.

Checkpoint 1

Do They Need to Act?

The Urgency Test · Questions 1–4

If you get this wrong, nothing else matters.

Question 1 · The Core Problem
"What is the exact problem they are trying to solve?"

Why It's Asked

Prospects almost always open with a surface complaint. "Our ATS is clunky" isn't the real problem. You need to push past the symptom to the strategic initiative. If you can't state their problem in their own words, you cannot solve it.

What to Listen For

Three categories of answers: current problem/pain, future goal, or potential problem they want to get ahead of. If they have a goal, that means they have a problem keeping them from achieving it — or they already would have gotten there.

💡 Bonus question: "Walk me through what happens when you get a new application — start to finish." This reveals the real process breakdown behind the surface complaint.

Question 1b · The Priority Scale
"On a scale of 1–10, how critical is this initiative right now?"

Why It's Asked

Turns a vague feeling into a concrete number that commits them psychologically. It also gives you a baseline to reference later: "You said this was an 8 out of 10. Has anything changed?"

How to Use It

If they say 4 or below — pump the brakes. That's a signal to discover-and-disqualify, not push harder. If 8+, you have real urgency — document it and reference it throughout the deal.

Question 2 · The Stakeholder Map
"Who actually owns this problem and makes the decision?"

Why It's Asked

Selling to someone who cannot buy is wasted time. You need the economic buyer identified — and ideally on the call — as early as possible. "Who else cares about this?" stays connected to THEIR objective.

How to Deploy It

Ask: "Can we bring them in — even for 15 minutes?" If key stakeholders aren't present, ask: "What does [decision-maker] think about that?" to get a well-rounded picture. Deals without a mapped decision-maker close at very low rates.

💡 Bonus: "Are you exploring other solutions? It helps me focus on what matters most to you." If they say "just you" — push back. That usually means they're not being honest about where they are in the process.

Question 3 · The Cost of Inaction
"What happens if they do nothing?"

Why It's Asked

This is the highest-leverage question in CP1. Pain drives action. If doing nothing is acceptable, they will do nothing. You must make the status quo feel expensive and dangerous.

How to Amplify

Push into categories of business disruption: Time/Productivity, Money/Revenue, Quality/Performance, Risk/Compliance, Growth. The "worst-case scenario" version is the most powerful — it forces them to imagine the disaster in their own words.

💡 If they downplay it: "True, you manage — but what toll is it taking? Is it causing overtime or lost clients?" If they truly can't identify impact → they may fail CP1 → consider disqualifying.

Question 4 · Priority Level
"Is fixing this a top priority for them right now?"

Why It's Asked

A real problem that isn't urgent isn't a deal. Even great solutions won't sell without urgency. "Why is this a priority now vs. 6 months from now?" forces them to articulate the trigger event.

When You Skip This

Without a trigger event, deals rot in pipeline. "Why is this a priority now vs. 6 months from now?" forces them to articulate the urgency. If they can't — they don't have it, and no amount of follow-up will create it.

✅ CP1 Prevents: Timing and Competition fatalities. If you pass CP1, the prospect has confirmed: something is burning, the right people feel it, and they need to act NOW.

When Checkpoint 1 Fails

DOA: Wrong Timing

"It's just not that bad right now."

If they never fully agree they need to act now, they lose steam. Your proposal sits ignored. The deal rots.

Without urgency, even a great solution won't sell. You failed to uncover the pain or they rationalized it away.

Do not advance to D2 without explicit, documented urgency.

Checkpoint 2

Do They Need Outside Help?

The Gap Test · Questions 5–8

Just because they have a problem doesn't mean they'll buy a solution.

Question 5 · Current Approach
"What are they currently doing to try and fix this?"

Why It's Asked

Understand the baseline. If they've invested before and it failed, you can leverage those specific failures. If it's their first time — understand what held them back. Every prior attempt = ammunition.

Three Common Answers

1. Self-repair: They've tried to fix it internally. 2. Another vendor: They're using a competitor that isn't working. 3. New problem: They haven't tried anything yet. Each answer requires a different follow-up strategy.

💡 Bonus: "How many separate tools are involved in your hiring process?" Let them name every tool — each one is a pain multiplier. "What if it were just 1?"

Question 6 · The Capability Gap
"Why have their current efforts failed?"

Why It's Asked

Establish that they cannot fix the problem on their own. Root causes matter. "Where does the problem BEGIN in your process?" Then: "What % of candidates make it to a first interview?"

The Insanity Frame

"If your existing resources could have solved this, they would have. But it still isn't solved. So what are we going to do about it?" This is the logic that breaks the status quo trance and creates the gap.

⚠️ Critical: "What HRIS or payroll system do you use? Does data need to flow into it?" Integration is the #1 deal-blocker AND #1 retention play. Ask in the first 10 minutes — don't discover it in D4.

The Most Critical Question in the Entire BAP

"Based on what we've discussed, do you feel your team has the tools and bandwidth to solve this in the timeline you need?"

Why It's The One

They must explicitly admit they can't solve this alone. If they don't say it, the gap isn't real. Pitching before this admission leads to status quo losses — they'll default to doing nothing because you never proved they need help.

The Scale Version

"On a scale of 1–10, how capable is your team of solving this alone?" Once they say "3 out of 10" — they've committed. If 8+, push back: "Then why are we talking?"

Deals die here when the prospect only needs a partial solution — not a full platform. If the gap isn't real, disqualify. Don't force it.

Question 7 · The Buying Process
"How exactly do they buy things like this?"

Why It's Asked

Knowing their process prevents late-stage surprises. Multi-step procurement can add 3–6 months. Ask for security questionnaires upfront. We're ISO 27001/SOC2 ready — use that as a weapon.

How to Deploy It

Ask for security questionnaires upfront. We're ISO 27001/SOC2 ready — use that as a weapon. Also ask: "Do you typically budget for improvements like this, or only allocate funds when something goes wrong?" — reactive vs. proactive tells you everything.

Question 8 · Readiness
"Are they capable of implementing a solution now?"

Why It's Asked

Even if they want it, can they absorb it? Budget, bandwidth, timeline. Be radically honest: "I need you to be hiring 5+ people/year for this to save you money."

The Honesty Play

Be radically transparent: "Money loves speed. Time kills all deals." Ask when they want work completed → calculate when the contract needs to be signed → now you have a deadline tied to THEIR goals, not yours. Honesty about pricing builds trust faster than discounting.

✅ CP2 Prevents: Competition and Price fatalities. If you pass CP2, the prospect has confirmed: their internal resources are insufficient, and they know they need outside help.

Checkpoint 2 Strategy

The Problem Reframe

Don't agree with their diagnosis. Reframe it into a bigger problem that only WE can solve.

❌ Their Diagnosis

"Our recruiters are spending too much time on manual screening and sourcing."

If you solve this at face value, any ATS vendor can win.

✅ The Reframe

"Your TA team spends 60% of their time on manual sourcing and screening instead of closing — that's a capacity crisis that compounds every quarter."

Cost frame: "recruiter capacity gap" → Reframed around TT's automation + AI.

Reveal Question: "If your recruiters could reclaim 15 hours per week from manual tasks, how many more hires would they close?" — then let the silence work.

Checkpoint 2 Strategy

Reframe by Persona

Match the reframe to who you're talking to. Each one names a different disease and cost frame.

PersonaThe Bigger ProblemReveal QuestionCost Frame
VP of TA "Your TA team spends 60% of their time on manual sourcing and screening instead of closing — that's a capacity crisis that compounds every quarter." "If your recruiters could reclaim 15 hours/week from manual tasks, how many more hires would they close?" Recruiter Capacity Gap
CHRO / VP People "You're losing the talent war to competitors who move 3× faster — speed IS your competitive advantage, and every day of delay costs you your best candidates." "How many of your top-choice candidates accepted another offer before you made yours?" Competitive Speed Gap
Dir. Employer Brand "You're invisible to the 70% of talent that's passive — every competitor with a stronger employer brand is winning candidates you never even see." "When was the last time you measured how many qualified candidates abandoned your career site before applying?" Talent Brand Gap
Hiring Managers "Every day a hiring manager can't self-serve in your system, you add 5 days to time-to-fill — and your best candidates are gone in 10." "What % of your hiring managers actually log into your ATS more than once a month?" Manager Adoption Gap
Leadership / Execs "Your hiring infrastructure wasn't built for the speed and scale your business needs today — you're solving symptoms instead of the root cause." "If you could redesign your entire hiring process from scratch, what's the first thing you'd fix?" Infrastructure Gap
Checkpoint 2 Strategy

The Reverse Timeline Trap

Don't ask when they'll sign. Put the deadline entirely on them.

  • 01Get the Go-Live Date — "When do you need this fully rolled out and live for the team?"
  • 02Inject Implementation Time — "Our implementation takes exactly 3 weeks to do it right."
  • 03Set the Trap — "Working backward, to hit your goal, we need the contract signed by [date]. Can we meet that deadline?"

"You didn't set a deadline. They did. You just did the math for them."

When Checkpoint 2 Fails

DOA: Competition

"We can just handle this internally."

If you don't prove their existing resources are insufficient, they default to status quo. "Our team can handle it."

The Dunning-Kruger effect — unskilled people overestimate their ability to solve complex problems. They think they're wise.

You MUST surface the gap before advancing to D3.

Checkpoint 3

Are We the Best Solution?

The Solution Test · Questions 9–11

They need to act. They need outside help. Will they pick us?

Question 9 · Proven Results
"Have we proven we can solve this specific problem?"

Why It's Asked

Case studies eliminate risk. The prospect needs to see proof — not promises. If you can't show you've solved this exact problem for a similar company, you're asking them to take a leap of faith.

How to Deploy It

Lead with platform stats (97% see increase in qualified candidates, 89% say hiring is faster, 12,000+ companies). Then match a case study to their industry/size. Close with: "Would you like to speak directly with one of our clients?"

Question 10 · Solution Map
"Is it completely clear how our product fixes their problem?"

Why It's Asked

Connect the dots explicitly. Every click in the demo must tie to their specific pain. Generic demos lose. The formula: "You told me [THEIR WORDS]... Here's how we solve that... Here's what happened when [similar company] used this..."

The Demo Formula

"You told me [THEIR WORDS]... Here's how we solve that... Here's what happened when [similar company] used this."

Every click in the demo must tie to their specific pain. Generic demos lose. Structure around the cost frame from your diagnosis, not a feature tour.

Question 11 · Success Metrics
"How will they measure if this was a success?"

Why It's Asked

Set clear ROI expectations before the proposal. This becomes the anchor for the Confirmation Close. If you can't quantify the win, they can't justify the investment internally.

Common Success Metrics

Time-to-fill ↓ · Cost-per-hire ↓ · Application volume ↑ · Agency spend ↓ · Hiring manager adoption · Career site traffic · Quality of applicants

✅ CP3 Prevents: Price, Trust, and Product Fit fatalities. If you pass CP3, you've proven we're the best option, mapped the solution to their pain, and set measurable success criteria.

Checkpoint 3 Strategy

Arming the Champion

The champion sells when you're not in the room.

Never let them walk in empty-handed.

  • If the champion can't articulate the solution internally, the deal dies in committee.
  • Share the Discovery Room link after your demo — it's their internal selling tool.
  • It gives them exactly what they need: ROI, case studies, and proof that we solve their specific pain.

"How can I help you build the internal case?" → "How can I show you that value so you can take it to [name] and say 'we want to spend this money'?"

When Checkpoint 3 Fails

DOA: Price · Trust · Product Fit

💰 Price

"It's too expensive." — You failed to quantify ROI. No value frame = sticker shock = lost deal.

🤝 Trust

"We're not sure about you." — Trust dies through pushy tactics and dodging hard questions. Credibility is earned through candor.

🧩 Product Fit

"It doesn't do what we need." — No unique diagnosis = no differentiation. Generic demos lose to any competitor.

Part 4

Progression Pathways

The deal is dying. Here's how you fight back.
Every fatality has a countermeasure.

The Objection Map

Fatality → Missed Checkpoint → Countermeasure

FatalityThey Say...Missed CPPathway
1. Timing "Let's revisit next quarter" CP1 — Urgency Pain / Priority
2. Competition "We went with someone else" CP2 — Gap Problem Reframe + Gap in Ability
3. Price "It's too expensive" CP3 — Solution Quantifiable Outcomes (ROI)
4. Trust "We're not sure about you" CP3 — Solution Proven Results
5. Product Fit "It doesn't do what we need" CP2 — Gap Problem Reframe

Diagnose the fatality. Go back to the missed checkpoint. Apply the pathway. Advance.

Pathway 1 · Kills: Timing → Go Back to CP1
Pain / Priority

The Psychology

Push/Pull Inversion: When you try to pull prospects toward you, they push away. Flip it — use "discover and disqualify" posture. Say: "This doesn't seem like it's causing that much of a problem. Should we just reconnect in 30 days?" They'll say: "No, actually, here's what's really going on."

Rationalization Cycle: When pain isn't clear, prospects rationalize → justify → avoid → ghost. "First world problems" thinking kicks in and the deal slides down the priority list.

Tactical Execution

Step 1: Acknowledge — "I understand this might not seem like a top priority right now."

Step 2: Amplify — "What's the worst-case scenario if this doesn't get addressed?" (Most powerful question — forces them to imagine disaster in their own words)

Step 3: Quantify — "What does every unfilled role cost you per month?"

Step 4: Confirm — "Just to recap: you're dealing with [problem], which is causing [impacts]. Does that sound right?"

💡 If they downplay: "True, you manage — but what toll is it taking? Is it causing overtime or lost clients?" If they truly can't identify impact → they may fail CP1 → disqualify.

Pathway 2 · Kills: Competition & Product Fit → Go Back to CP2
Problem Reframe

The Psychology

"If you're not diagnosing, you're just another vendor." When you solve the same surface-level problem anyone can solve, you commoditize yourself and win only through price.

The prospect's symptoms point to something larger. Your job: connect the dots between individual symptoms and the systemic disease. Then align that disease to Teamtailor's special sauce — the thing we do that nobody else can.

The 6-Step Reframe

1. Listen for surface symptoms they describe

2. Acknowledge — show you heard and understood

3. Deliver the bigger problem — use the reframe script

4. Ask the reveal question — let the silence work

5. Frame the demo around the cost frame, not features

6. Build tailored agenda based on selected pains

Example reframe: "Our ATS is clunky" → "The bigger issue is a clunky ATS destroys your Candidate Experience and Employer Brand — losing top talent before they even apply."

💡 Reveal Question Example (VP of TA): "If your recruiters could reclaim 15 hours/week from manual tasks, how many more hires would they close?" → Reframe: "recruiter capacity gap"

Pathway 3 · Kills: Competition → Go Back to CP2
Gap in Ability

The Psychology

Dunning-Kruger Effect: "The unskilled person thinks they are wise, but the wise one knows themselves to be unskilled." If we don't help prospects see their resources are insufficient, we allow them to think they can fix this alone.

The Insanity Frame: "If your existing resources could have solved this, they would have. But it still isn't solved. So what are we going to do about it?"

Fear of Change: People stick with imperfect situations because they're familiar. You must break the "status quo trance" with gentle logic.

Tactical Execution

Step 1: Identify current solution — "Are you working with another provider?"

Step 2: Surface the contradiction — "If your current solution is doing a great job, why are you looking for another one?"

Step 3: Make the gap obvious — "It sounds like if you continue relying on the current approach that hasn't fixed this, it's unlikely things will change."

Step 4: Position the bridge — Share ONE specific story: "A client's in-house team kept missing underlying issues. We fixed in weeks a problem that lingered for a year."

💡 Discover & Disqualify: If they don't need outside help, they may be: getting compliance bids, leveraging your proposal to negotiate with current vendor, or planning to hand your bid to their maintenance team. Protect your time.

Pathway 4 · Kills: Price → Go Back to CP3
Quantifiable Outcomes (ROI)

The Psychology

Investment vs. Cost: When the prospect sets the baseline with THEIR numbers, the output feels credible and personal — not hypothetical. Let them adjust the sliders.

Speed = Money: "Every day a role sits empty costs real money." Vacancy cost is the largest value layer and creates urgency around faster time-to-hire.

The anchoring effect: Show $18K/year investment is a fraction of $351K projected value. The 19.5× multiplier reframes "expense" as "massive return."

The 3-Layer Value Stack

Layer 1 — Software Savings: HMs × $1,200/yr (legacy charges per seat — we don't)

Layer 2 — Agency Reduction: 25% organic shift × $15K per avoided placement

Layer 3 — Vacancy Recovery: Total hires × days saved × $400/day lost productivity

Example: 15 HMs + 60 hires/yr + 30-day TTH → $18K + $45K + $288K = $351K value · 19.5× ROI

💡 Ask: "If I showed you that a $30K investment would save $200K in agency fees this year — what would you need to believe for that math to be credible?" Let them set the bar, then exceed it with their own numbers.

Pathway 5 · Kills: Trust → Go Back to CP3
Proven Results

The Psychology

Amazon Reviews Analogy: "Think about your own behavior on Amazon — you pick the item with the highest reviews and highest stars. That's proven results. It doesn't matter how good the marketing is."

Credibility BEFORE solution: Don't start with solution details. Start with establishing why you exist. What gap existed that said this company can do something no other company can?

"We've been in business 30 years" is NOT proof. Follow up with real metrics: "30 years and over 500 successful implementations." Longevity alone doesn't close deals.

Tactical Execution

1. Origin Story: Why Teamtailor exists — the gap we fill

2. Battle-Tested Stats: 97% see increase in qualified candidates · 89% say hiring is faster · 94% collaborate better · 12,000+ companies

3. Matched Case Study: Nobu Hotel cut TTH by 40%. ESTO reduced to 21 days. Giacom got 70 referrals. Five Guys automated 100% of responses.

4. The Offer: "Would you like to speak directly with one of our clients in [their industry]?"

Paint the dream-like vision: "Psychologically it's very powerful to put them there so they can see 'this is going to be a really good partnership.'"

💡 The "No Maybes" rule: After presenting proof — "A yes is fine. A no is fine. Maybe is never okay." Drive to a decision. Ambiguity kills deals.

The Rubric

How the BAP Scores

8–10

YES

Checkpoint passed. Buyer fully aligned. Green light to advance to next phase.

5–7

MAYBE

You have gaps. Do NOT advance. Clarify the remaining BAP questions first.

1–4

NO

Deal is blocked. Disqualify or nurture. If you pitch here, you will lose.

"If a deal passes all 3 checkpoints, you have systematically eliminated every reason it could die. That's not hope. That's engineering."

D4 — Before the Proposal

The 5 Alignment Checks

Confirmation Close. Verify all 5 before sending any proposal. No exceptions.

#Alignment CheckWhat You Ask
1 Urgent Priority? "Do you agree this is urgent enough to act on now?"
2 Resources Insufficient? "Do you agree your current resources can't solve it?"
3 Problems & Solutions Aligned? "Are you clear on how we solve these problems?"
4 ROI Sufficient? "Does the ROI justify the investment?"
5 Are We the Right Solution? "Have you confirmed Teamtailor is your preferred option?"

If YES → "We're looking forward to partnering with you."
If HESITATION → "We're aligned on the problems. What part of our solution is giving you pause?"

Command Center

The Discovery Room

The BAP isn't a spreadsheet. It lives inside the Discovery Room for every active deal.

  • 01One Source of Truth — Use the Discovery Room for every active deal. No exceptions.
  • 02BAP Scorecard — Houses your BAP answers, quantifies pain, enforces toll booths between stages.
  • 03Solution Pillars — Mechanically maps their selected pain points to our Solution Pillars and ROI.
  • 04Champion's Weapon — Share the link after your demo. It's how they sell internally when you're not in the room.
The System

The Logic Chain

Every deal that dies traces to one of 5 fatalities.
Every fatality traces to a missed checkpoint.
Every missed checkpoint traces to unanswered BAP questions.
Therefore: Answer all 11 → Pass all 3 → Prevent all 5 → Close the deal.

Summary

The Playbook

Step 1
Ask the Questions
Find the fire, expose the gap
→
Step 2
Score the BAP
Red, yellow, or green?
→
Step 3
Arm the Champion
Send the Discovery Room
→
Outcome
✅ Deal Closes
Engineered, not hoped

"The Buyer's Action Plan removes guesswork from sales. It tells you where you stand, what you're missing, and what to do next."

Now let's look at your deals and score them.

1 / 40 The 3 Checkpoints — Deep Dive ← → keys · click
